name: Privilege Escalation
title: Suspicious service installation and privilege use on {server} by {user}
description: A new service pointing to a user-writable path was installed on {server} (EventCode 7045), followed by the account {user} receiving special privileges (4672) it does not normally hold.

mitre.name: Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service

objective: Determine whether the service installation on {server} is a privilege-escalation mechanism, and whether {user}'s new privilege level has been abused.
checklist: Locate the 7045 service-installation event and examine the image path
Assess the service binary location — system path or user-writable directory?
Find the 4672 special-privileges event and compare against {user}'s normal entitlements
Review what ran under the elevated context afterward
Check how {user}'s session reached {server} in the first place
hints: Services installed by software deployments point to C:\Program Files or C:\Windows. This one points to a Temp directory — services don't belong there.
4672 fires at logon for admins. The anomaly is not the event itself, but this account receiving SeDebugPrivilege when it is a standard user account.
Standard escalation chain: land as a user, install a service that runs as SYSTEM, use the SYSTEM shell for whatever comes next. Look at the child processes of the service binary.
expectedEvidence: 7045 service installation with a user-writable image path
4672 special privileges assigned to a normally-standard account
Process activity under the elevated context
explanation: This is privilege escalation via malicious service creation (T1543.003), one of the oldest and still most common Windows escalation techniques: services run as SYSTEM, so an attacker who can install one converts user-level access into full control. The forensic tells: the service image lives in a Temp folder (deployment tools never do this), the service name mimics a legitimate one with a slight misspelling, and a standard user account suddenly holds SeDebugPrivilege. From here an attacker typically dumps credentials or establishes persistence, so the host must be treated as fully compromised — rebuild rather than clean.
reportTemplate: 1. Isolate {server}. 2. Treat all credentials used on {server} since the escalation as compromised — reset them. 3. Capture the service binary from Temp for analysis. 4. Review lateral movement FROM {server} after the 4672 event. 5. Plan a rebuild of the host.

events.message: An account was successfully logged on. Account Name: {user} Logon Type: 3 Source Network Address: {src_ip}

A service was installed in the system. Service Name: WinDefendUpdtr Service File Name: C:\Users\{user}\AppData\Local\Temp\wdupdater.exe Service Type: user mode service Service Start Type: auto start Service Account: LocalSystem
Special privileges assigned to new logon. Account Name: {user} Privileges: SeDebugPrivilege, SeTcbPrivilege, SeImpersonatePrivilege
Process Create: Image: C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe CommandLine: cmd.exe /c whoami /priv ParentImage: C:\Users\{user}\AppData\Local\Temp\wdupdater.exe User: NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM
